expanded_description:
Global financial crises are severe disruptions in financial markets that spread across countries through contagion, causing sharp declines in asset prices, bank failures, currency collapses, and deep recessions. Major episodes include the Great Depression (1929-1939), the Latin American debt crisis (1980s), the Asian financial crisis (1997-1998), the Russian financial crisis (1998), the Argentine crisis (2001-2002), the Global Financial Crisis (2007-2009), and the COVID-19 pandemic crisis (2020). These crises share common features: excessive risk-taking, asset bubbles, high leverage, capital flow volatility, and weak regulation. Contagion occurs when a crisis in one country spreads to others through trade linkages, financial linkages, or investor panic (herd behavior).

Understanding global financial crises is essential for analyzing the causes of economic downturns, evaluating the effectiveness of crisis prevention and resolution policies, and designing international financial architecture (IMF, World Bank, Basel Accords). The 2008 Global Financial Crisis (GFC) was the most severe since the Great Depression, leading to the collapse of Lehman Brothers, bailouts of major banks, and a global recession. The crisis revealed weaknesses in financial regulation (shadow banking, derivatives, subprime mortgages) and led to major reforms (Dodd-Frank, Basel III).

In this section, we will examine the causes of financial crises (asset bubbles, leverage, maturity mismatch, currency mismatches, capital flow volatility), analyze contagion mechanisms (trade links, financial links, investor panic), study major crises (Asian financial crisis, Global Financial Crisis, European debt crisis), and evaluate policy responses (lender of last resort, bailouts, fiscal stimulus, monetary easing, capital controls). You will learn to identify early warning signs of crises, understand the role of the IMF in crisis resolution, and evaluate reforms to prevent future crises.

core_concept.principle:
Global financial crises are caused by a combination of factors: asset bubbles, excessive leverage, maturity mismatch, currency mismatches, capital flow volatility, and weak regulation. Contagion spreads crises across countries through trade, financial linkages, and investor panic.

Common Causes of Financial Crises:

• Asset bubbles: Rapid increase in asset prices (housing, stocks) fueled by easy credit, eventually crashing.
• Excessive leverage: Borrowing to invest, amplifying gains and losses.
• Maturity mismatch: Banks borrow short-term (deposits) and lend long-term (mortgages), creating rollover risk.
• Currency mismatch: Borrowing in foreign currency (e.g., USD) while earning in local currency, creating exchange rate risk.
• Capital flow volatility: Sudden stops or reversals of capital inflows cause currency crises.
• Weak regulation: Inadequate supervision of banks, shadow banking, derivatives.
• Moral hazard: Expectation of bailouts encourages excessive risk-taking.

Contagion Mechanisms:

• Trade linkages: A crisis in one country reduces demand for exports from trading partners.
• Financial linkages: Banks exposed to a crisis country suffer losses, leading to credit contraction.
• Investor panic (herd behavior): Investors withdraw from countries perceived as similar (wake-up call).
• Competitive devaluation: Countries devalue to maintain export competitiveness, spreading currency crises.

Major Crises:

• Asian financial crisis (1997-1998): Currency and banking crises in Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, Philippines. Causes: fixed exchange rates, large foreign currency debt, weak banking systems. Contagion spread to Russia and Brazil.
• Global Financial Crisis (2007-2009): US subprime mortgage crisis spread to global banking system. Causes: housing bubble, securitization, shadow banking, excessive leverage, weak regulation. Lehman Brothers collapsed (2008); bailouts of AIG, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac; TARP (\$700 billion). Contagion spread to Europe (PIIGS).
• European debt crisis (2010-2015): Sovereign debt crises in Greece, Ireland, Portugal, Spain, Italy. Causes: high public debt, weak growth, banking losses from GFC. Bailouts by EU/IMF (\$300 billion).

Policy Responses:

• Lender of last resort: Central bank provides liquidity to solvent banks (discount window, emergency facilities).
• Bailouts: Government injects capital into troubled banks (TARP).
• Fiscal stimulus: Government spending to boost aggregate demand (ARRA 2009).
• Monetary easing: Interest rate cuts, QE, forward guidance.
• Capital controls: Restrict capital outflows to prevent currency collapse (Malaysia 1998).
• IMF programs: Conditional loans to crisis countries (austerity, structural reforms).

Prevention and Reforms:

• Basel III: Higher capital requirements, liquidity coverage ratio, leverage ratio.
• Dodd-Frank Act (2010): Volcker Rule (restrict proprietary trading), stress tests, orderly liquidation authority.
• Macroprudential regulation: Countercyclical capital buffers, loan-to-value limits.
• IMF surveillance: Early warning systems, Article IV consultations.
• Financial safety nets: Swap lines (Fed with other central banks), regional arrangements (CMIM, ESM).
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine a country with a fixed exchange rate, banks borrowing in US dollars (cheap) and lending in local currency (high interest). Foreign investors pour money in, chasing high returns. Asset prices (real estate, stocks) soar. Then something triggers a reversal: maybe the US raises interest rates, or investors lose confidence. Capital flows reverse; investors sell local currency to buy dollars. The central bank must defend the fixed exchange rate by selling dollars, but its reserves are limited. It runs out; the currency collapses. Banks that borrowed in dollars now owe more in local currency; they go bankrupt. The economy plunges into recession. This is the Asian financial crisis of 1997.

Contagion spreads because investors panic. They see Thailand in trouble and sell the currencies of Indonesia, South Korea, and Malaysia, even though those countries have different fundamentals. This is herd behavior. Trade linkages also spread the crisis: when Thailand imports less, its neighbors export less.

The Global Financial Crisis (2008) was different. It started in the US housing market, not a developing country. Banks had lent to subprime borrowers (poor credit history) and packaged those loans into securities (mortgage-backed securities, CDOs). When housing prices fell, borrowers defaulted; the securities became worthless. Banks that held them (Lehman Brothers, Bear Stearns, AIG) faced collapse. The US government bailed out AIG and others but let Lehman fail, triggering a global panic. The crisis spread to Europe (Ireland, Greece, Portugal, Spain, Italy).

Policy responses included lender of last resort (Fed discount window, emergency facilities), bailouts (TARP, \$700 billion), fiscal stimulus (ARRA, \$800 billion), and monetary easing (QE, zero interest rates). The IMF provided loans to crisis countries (Greece, Ireland, Portugal) with conditions (austerity).

Reforms after the crisis included Basel III (higher capital requirements), Dodd-Frank (Volcker Rule, stress tests), and macroprudential regulation (countercyclical buffers). These aim to prevent future crises, but critics argue they are insufficient.

For Canada, the banking system remained stable during the GFC due to conservative regulation (high capital requirements, low leverage). Canada did not have a housing bubble (though housing prices later rose). The Bank of Canada cut rates and provided liquidity; the government provided fiscal stimulus. Canada weathered the crisis better than most developed countries.

image.alt: Timeline: Great Depression (1929), Latin American debt crisis (1980s), Asian financial crisis (1997), Russian crisis (1998), Argentine crisis (2001), Global Financial Crisis (2008), European debt crisis (2010), COVID-19 crisis (2020).
image.caption: Figure 14.5.1: Major global financial crises since the Great Depression. Each crisis has unique features but shares common causes: asset bubbles, leverage, capital flow volatility, and weak regulation.

sections.introduction:
Global financial crises are severe disruptions in financial markets that spread across countries through contagion, causing sharp declines in asset prices, bank failures, currency collapses, and deep recessions. Major episodes include the Great Depression (1929-1939), the Latin American debt crisis (1980s), the Asian financial crisis (1997-1998), the Russian financial crisis (1998), the Argentine crisis (2001-2002), the Global Financial Crisis (2007-2009), and the COVID-19 pandemic crisis (2020). These crises share common features: excessive risk-taking, asset bubbles, high leverage, capital flow volatility, and weak regulation. Contagion occurs when a crisis in one country spreads to others through trade linkages, financial linkages, or investor panic (herd behavior).

Understanding global financial crises is essential for analyzing the causes of economic downturns, evaluating the effectiveness of crisis prevention and resolution policies, and designing international financial architecture (IMF, World Bank, Basel Accords). The 2008 Global Financial Crisis (GFC) was the most severe since the Great Depression, leading to the collapse of Lehman Brothers, bailouts of major banks, and a global recession. The crisis revealed weaknesses in financial regulation (shadow banking, derivatives, subprime mortgages) and led to major reforms (Dodd-Frank, Basel III).

In this section, we will examine the causes of financial crises (asset bubbles, leverage, maturity mismatch, currency mismatches, capital flow volatility), analyze contagion mechanisms (trade links, financial links, investor panic), study major crises (Asian financial crisis, Global Financial Crisis, European debt crisis), and evaluate policy responses (lender of last resort, bailouts, fiscal stimulus, monetary easing, capital controls).

introduction_image.alt: Timeline of major crises with GDP growth (global) showing sharp drops during each crisis.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 14.5.2: Global GDP growth has been disrupted by major financial crises. The Great Depression and the Global Financial Crisis saw the deepest contractions.

key_concept.definition:
Financial Crisis: A severe disruption in financial markets characterized by sharp declines in asset prices, bank failures, currency collapses, and credit contraction.

Common Causes:

• Asset bubbles: Rapid price increases fueled by easy credit, eventually crashing.
• Excessive leverage: Borrowing to invest, amplifying gains and losses.
• Maturity mismatch: Banks borrow short-term, lend long-term (rollover risk).
• Currency mismatch: Borrowing in foreign currency, earning in local currency (exchange rate risk).
• Capital flow volatility: Sudden stops or reversals of capital inflows.
• Weak regulation: Inadequate supervision of banks, shadow banking, derivatives.
• Moral hazard: Expectation of bailouts encourages excessive risk-taking.

Contagion Mechanisms:

• Trade linkages: Crisis reduces demand for trading partners' exports.
• Financial linkages: Banks exposed to crisis country suffer losses.
• Investor panic (herd behavior): Withdrawal from similar countries (wake-up call).
• Competitive devaluation: Countries devalue to maintain export competitiveness.

Major Crises:

• Asian financial crisis (1997-1998): Currency and banking crises in Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, Philippines. Causes: fixed exchange rates, large foreign currency debt, weak banking systems. Contagion to Russia, Brazil.
• Global Financial Crisis (2007-2009): US subprime mortgage crisis spread to global banking system. Causes: housing bubble, securitization, shadow banking, excessive leverage. Lehman collapse (2008); bailouts of AIG, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac; TARP (\$700B). Contagion to Europe (PIIGS).
• European debt crisis (2010-2015): Sovereign debt crises in Greece, Ireland, Portugal, Spain, Italy. Causes: high public debt, weak growth, banking losses from GFC. Bailouts by EU/IMF (\$300B).

Policy Responses:

• Lender of last resort: Central bank provides liquidity to solvent banks.
• Bailouts: Government injects capital into troubled banks (TARP).
• Fiscal stimulus: Government spending to boost aggregate demand (ARRA).
• Monetary easing: Interest rate cuts, QE, forward guidance.
• Capital controls: Restrict capital outflows to prevent currency collapse.
• IMF programs: Conditional loans to crisis countries (austerity, reforms).

Prevention and Reforms:

• Basel III: Higher capital requirements, liquidity coverage ratio, leverage ratio.
• Dodd-Frank Act (2010): Volcker Rule, stress tests, orderly liquidation authority.
• Macroprudential regulation: Countercyclical capital buffers, loan-to-value limits.
• IMF surveillance: Early warning systems, Article IV consultations.
• Financial safety nets: Swap lines (Fed), regional arrangements (CMIM, ESM).
key_concept.context: Financial crises have occurred throughout history (Tulip mania 1637, South Sea Bubble 1720, Panic of 1907). The study of financial crises intensified after the Great Depression (Kindleberger, Minsky, Reinhart and Rogoff). The Asian financial crisis (1997) led to reforms in international financial architecture. The Global Financial Crisis (2008) was the most severe since the Depression, leading to major regulatory reforms (Dodd-Frank, Basel III). The European debt crisis (2010-2015) revealed flaws in the Eurozone architecture (no fiscal union). The COVID-19 crisis (2020) was unique: a health crisis that triggered a financial crisis, but swift policy responses prevented a full-blown depression. For Canada, the banking system remained stable during the GFC due to conservative regulation, but Canada was affected by the global recession.

image.alt: Graph: Exchange rates (USD/local currency) for Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea (1997-1998); sharp depreciations.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.3: The Asian financial crisis (1997-1998) saw sharp currency depreciations in Thailand, Indonesia, and South Korea. Contagion spread rapidly across the region.

classifications.value: Currency and banking crises in Southeast Asia triggered by capital flow reversal, fixed exchange rates, and foreign currency debt
characteristics: Fixed exchange rates (pegged to USD).
Large foreign currency debt (borrowed in USD, earned in local currency).
Weak banking systems (poor regulation, connected lending).
Capital flow reversal: foreign investors withdrew, causing currency collapse.
Contagion spread to Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, Philippines, then Russia, Brazil.
IMF provided bailout packages (\$40B to Thailand, \$50B to Indonesia, \$58B to South Korea).
classifications.behavior:
The Asian financial crisis began in Thailand in July 1997 when the baht collapsed after the government ran out of foreign reserves defending the peg. The crisis spread to Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, and the Philippines. Contagion occurred through trade linkages (Thailand's imports fell) and financial linkages (banks exposed to Thai debt). Investors panicked and withdrew from all 'Asian tiger' economies, regardless of fundamentals.

The underlying causes were: (1) fixed exchange rates that encouraged borrowing in foreign currency; (2) large current account deficits; (3) weak banking systems with poor regulation; (4) excessive short-term foreign currency debt; (5) asset bubbles (real estate, stocks).

The IMF provided bailout packages with conditions: raise interest rates (to defend currency), cut spending (reduce deficit), close weak banks, and implement structural reforms (liberalize trade, improve governance). These policies were controversial; critics argued that high interest rates worsened the recession (austerity).

South Korea recovered relatively quickly after restructuring chaebols (conglomerates) and banks. Indonesia suffered a deeper and longer crisis due to political instability. Malaysia rejected IMF conditions, imposed capital controls (restricting outflows), and recovered faster than its neighbors.

For Canada, the crisis had limited direct impact but reduced demand for Canadian exports (commodities). The Bank of Canada cut rates to support growth.

image.alt: Graph: GDP growth in Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea (1996-2000); sharp drops in 1998.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.4: The Asian financial crisis caused deep recessions in affected countries. Indonesia's GDP fell by 13% in 1998; South Korea fell by 5%.

examples.explanation: Thailand had a fixed exchange rate (25 baht per USD). Foreign investors lent baht, converted to dollars, invested in high-yield assets. When the US raised interest rates, investors pulled out, selling baht for dollars. The central bank used reserves to defend the peg but ran out. The baht floated and fell by 50%. Banks that had borrowed in dollars went bankrupt. The economy contracted by 10% in 1998.

Malaysia rejected IMF conditions and imposed capital controls (restricting outflows). The ringgit was pegged at 3.80 per USD. Capital controls prevented panic selling. Malaysia recovered faster than its neighbors, and the controls were eventually lifted. This challenged the IMF's orthodoxy.

classifications.value: The most severe crisis since the Great Depression; triggered by US subprime mortgage collapse, spreading through shadow banking and derivatives
characteristics: US housing bubble: prices doubled (2000-2006), then fell 30% (2007-2009).
Subprime mortgages: loans to borrowers with poor credit history, often adjustable-rate.
Securitization: mortgages packaged into MBS, CDOs, sold to investors worldwide.
Shadow banking: non-bank financial institutions (investment banks, hedge funds, money market funds) with little regulation.
Excessive leverage: investment banks leveraged 30:1 (\$30 assets per \$1 equity).
Derivatives: credit default swaps (CDS) insured MBS; counterparty risk.
Lehman Brothers collapsed (September 15, 2008), triggering global panic.
classifications.behavior:
The Global Financial Crisis (GFC) originated in the US housing market. Easy credit, low interest rates, and lax lending standards fueled a housing bubble. Subprime mortgages (loans to borrowers with poor credit) were packaged into mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and collateralized debt obligations (CDOs) and sold to investors worldwide. Rating agencies gave these securities AAA ratings (low risk), but they were actually very risky.

When housing prices fell, subprime borrowers defaulted. MBS and CDO values collapsed. Banks that held them (Lehman Brothers, Bear Stearns, AIG) faced insolvency. The shadow banking system (investment banks, hedge funds, money market funds) was highly leveraged and unregulated. AIG had sold credit default swaps (CDS) insuring MBS; when MBS defaulted, AIG faced huge losses.

In March 2008, Bear Stearns was bailed out (sold to JP Morgan with Fed backing). In September 2008, the Treasury allowed Lehman Brothers to fail, triggering a global panic. Money market funds 'broke the buck' (Reserve Primary Fund). Credit markets froze; interbank lending stopped.

The US government responded with: (1) TARP (\$700 billion) to bail out banks; (2) Fed emergency facilities (PDCF, TSLF, CPFF, MMIFF) to provide liquidity; (3) zero interest rates and QE; (4) fiscal stimulus (ARRA, \$800 billion).

Contagion spread to Europe: banks had bought US MBS; Ireland, Greece, Portugal, Spain, Italy faced sovereign debt crises (European debt crisis). The global economy fell into the Great Recession (2008-2009), with GDP falling 5% in the US, 6% in Europe.

For Canada, the banking system remained stable due to conservative regulation (high capital requirements, low leverage, no subprime lending). However, Canada experienced a recession (GDP fell 3%) due to reduced demand for exports (US, commodities). The Bank of Canada cut rates to 0.25%; the government provided fiscal stimulus. Canada weathered the crisis better than most developed countries.

image.alt: Graph: US housing prices (Case-Shiller index) 2000-2010; sharp rise, then sharp fall.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.5: The US housing bubble (2000-2006) and bust (2007-2009) triggered the Global Financial Crisis. Housing prices fell by 30% nationally, more in some regions.

examples.explanation: Lehman Brothers was a large investment bank with \$600 billion in assets, leveraged 30:1. It held large amounts of MBS and CDOs. When those assets lost value, Lehman became insolvent. The Treasury and Fed refused to bail it out (moral hazard concerns). Lehman filed for bankruptcy on September 15, 2008, triggering a global panic. The lesson: letting a large institution fail can cause systemic collapse.

AIG had sold credit default swaps (CDS) insuring MBS. When MBS defaulted, AIG faced \$100 billion in losses. The Fed bailed out AIG with \$180 billion (taking an 80% equity stake). AIG was deemed 'too big to fail' because its failure would have brought down many counterparties (Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley). The bailout was controversial but prevented a worse collapse.

classifications.value: Sovereign debt crises in the Eurozone periphery triggered by the Global Financial Crisis, revealing flaws in the Eurozone architecture
characteristics: High public debt: Greece (120% of GDP), Italy (120%), Portugal (100%), Ireland (80%), Spain (60%).
Weak growth: Eurozone periphery had low productivity, high unemployment.
Banking losses: Banks in Ireland, Spain had invested in US MBS and local real estate.
Loss of market confidence: Investors demanded higher yields on government bonds.
No fiscal union: Eurozone has common monetary policy (ECB) but separate fiscal policies.
No lender of last resort for sovereigns (initially).
Bailouts by EU/IMF: Greece (\$300B), Ireland (\$100B), Portugal (\$100B), Spain (\$100B for banks).
classifications.behavior:
The European debt crisis began in 2010 when Greece revealed that its budget deficit was much larger than reported (12% of GDP vs. 3% EU limit). Investors lost confidence; Greek bond yields soared. Contagion spread to Ireland (banking crisis), Portugal, Spain, and Italy (the PIIGS). The Eurozone faced a sovereign debt crisis because it has a common currency but separate fiscal policies. There was no lender of last resort for sovereigns (the ECB was initially reluctant to buy government bonds).

The EU and IMF provided bailout packages with conditions (austerity): cut spending, raise taxes, privatize state assets, implement structural reforms. Austerity caused deep recessions: Greek GDP fell by 25% between 2008 and 2015; unemployment rose to 27%. This led to a humanitarian crisis and political backlash (rise of Syriza in Greece, Podemos in Spain).

The ECB eventually provided backstops: Outright Monetary Transactions (OMT, 2012) allowed unlimited bond purchases, calming markets. Mario Draghi's 'whatever it takes' speech was a turning point. The ECB also implemented negative interest rates and QE.

For Canada, the European debt crisis had limited direct impact but reduced global growth and demand for Canadian exports. The Bank of Canada kept rates low to support growth.

image.alt: Graph: Greek 10-year bond yields (2008-2015); spike to 30% in 2012, then fall after ECB intervention.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.6: Greek bond yields spiked to 30% during the European debt crisis, reflecting default risk. ECB intervention (OMT, 2012) brought yields down.

examples.explanation: Greece received \$300 billion in bailout loans from the EU and IMF. Conditions required austerity: cut public sector wages, pensions, and spending; raise taxes; privatize state assets. The economy contracted by 25%; unemployment rose to 27%; poverty increased. Critics argued that austerity was self-defeating (reducing GDP made debt harder to repay). Supporters argued that without austerity, Greece would have defaulted and left the euro.

In July 2012, ECB President Mario Draghi said: 'Within our mandate, the ECB is ready to do whatever it takes to preserve the euro. And believe me, it will be enough.' This speech signaled that the ECB would buy unlimited bonds of distressed countries (OMT program). Bond yields fell sharply, and the crisis subsided. This is a classic example of the power of central bank commitment.

principles.principle: Contagion Spreads Crises Across Countries Through Financial and Trade Linkages, and Investor Panic
principles.statement: A financial crisis in one country does not stay contained. Contagion occurs through three main channels: (1) trade linkages: a crisis reduces demand for imports, hurting trading partners; (2) financial linkages: banks exposed to the crisis country suffer losses, reducing lending; (3) investor panic: investors withdraw from countries perceived as similar (herd behavior, wake-up call). Contagion can be irrational: investors may sell the currency of a healthy country simply because it is in the same region as a crisis country. This happened during the Asian financial crisis (Thailand → Indonesia, South Korea) and the European debt crisis (Greece → Ireland, Portugal, Spain, Italy). Contagion is why international financial cooperation (IMF, central bank swap lines) is essential during crises. The Fed provided dollar swap lines to other central banks during the GFC and COVID-19 crisis, preventing a global liquidity crunch.
principles.application: Policymakers should monitor financial and trade linkages to anticipate contagion. The IMF's early warning system identifies vulnerable countries. Central banks should establish swap lines in advance to provide liquidity during crises. Capital controls can be used to prevent panic outflows (Malaysia 1998). The Fed's swap lines during the GFC and COVID-19 were crucial for global stability. For Canada, contagion from the US is a major risk due to deep trade and financial linkages.
principles.example: During the Asian financial crisis, Thailand's baht collapsed in July 1997. Within weeks, investors sold the currencies of Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia, and the Philippines, even though those countries had different fundamentals. This was herd behavior (wake-up call). Contagion spread to Russia and Brazil in 1998. The IMF provided bailouts to contain the crisis.

image.alt: Diagram: Contagion channels (trade, financial, investor panic) spreading from crisis country to others.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.7: Contagion spreads crises through trade linkages, financial linkages, and investor panic. International cooperation (IMF, swap lines) is essential to contain contagion.

principles.principle: The Global Financial Crisis Revealed That Shadow Banking and Derivatives Are Major Sources of Systemic Risk
principles.statement: The 2008 crisis showed that regulation of traditional banks is insufficient to prevent financial crises. The shadow banking system (investment banks, hedge funds, money market funds, special purpose vehicles) operated with little regulation, high leverage, and maturity mismatch. Derivatives (credit default swaps, CDOs) created interconnectedness and counterparty risk. When the housing bubble burst, the shadow banking system collapsed, triggering a global panic. Post-crisis reforms (Dodd-Frank, Basel III) extended regulation to shadow banking: money market funds now have liquidity requirements; derivatives must be cleared through central counterparties (CCPs); banks face higher capital and liquidity requirements. However, shadow banking has grown again, and new risks (crypto, stablecoins) have emerged. The lesson: regulation must keep pace with financial innovation.
principles.application: Regulators should monitor shadow banking and derivatives closely. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) coordinates international regulation. Central clearing of derivatives reduces counterparty risk but creates new risks (concentration at CCPs). Money market fund reform (liquidity requirements) has reduced run risk. For Canada, the shadow banking system is smaller than in the US, but Canadian banks have exposure to US shadow banking.
principles.example: AIG's sale of credit default swaps (CDS) is a classic example. AIG insured MBS without holding capital against the risk. When MBS defaulted, AIG faced \$100 billion in losses. The Fed bailed out AIG to prevent its failure from bringing down counterparties (Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley). After the crisis, regulations required central clearing of standardized derivatives and higher capital for OTC derivatives.

image.alt: Diagram: Shadow banking system (investment banks, hedge funds, money market funds, SPVs) and its linkages to traditional banks.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.8: The shadow banking system operates outside traditional bank regulation. Its interconnectedness with traditional banks creates systemic risk, as the 2008 crisis demonstrated.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Currency Crises and Reserves Adequacy

image.alt: Table: Country A (reserves $50B, short-term debt $40B, imports $20B/month). Calculate reserve adequacy ratios.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.9: Worked example: calculating reserve adequacy metrics to assess vulnerability to a currency crisis.

worked_examples.problem:
A country has the following data:

• Foreign exchange reserves: \$50 billion
• Short-term foreign currency debt (due within one year): \$40 billion
• Monthly imports: \$20 billion
• Broad money (M2): \$200 billion

a) Calculate the Greenspan-Guidotti rule (reserves / short-term debt). A ratio of 1.0 or higher is considered adequate.
b) Calculate the import cover (reserves / monthly imports). Traditional rule: 3 months of imports is adequate.
c) Calculate the Guidotti rule (reserves / broad money). Some economists use this to assess vulnerability to capital flight.
d) Based on these metrics, is the country vulnerable to a currency crisis?
e) If the country has a fixed exchange rate, how would your assessment change?

step_by_step.explanation:
Ratio = Reserves / Short-term debt = \$50B / \$40B = 1.25

This is above 1.0, so the country has enough reserves to cover short-term foreign currency debt. This suggests low vulnerability to a sudden stop (investors pulling out).

Ratio = Reserves / Monthly imports = \$50B / \$20B = 2.5 months

The traditional rule of thumb is 3 months of imports. This country has only 2.5 months, which is below the threshold. This suggests vulnerability if the country relies on imports for essential goods (food, energy).

Ratio = Reserves / M2 = \$50B / \$200B = 0.25 (25%)

This metric assesses vulnerability to capital flight (residents converting local currency to foreign currency). A ratio above 0.2 (20%) is often considered adequate. This country's ratio of 25% is above 20%, suggesting low vulnerability to capital flight.

The country is:
• Adequate on Greenspan-Guidotti (1.25 > 1.0).
• Below threshold on import cover (2.5 < 3 months).
• Adequate on reserves/M2 (25% > 20%).

The overall assessment is mixed. The country could withstand a sudden stop (investors pulling short-term debt) but might struggle if imports are disrupted. The country is moderately vulnerable but not in imminent danger.

If the country has a fixed exchange rate, reserve adequacy is even more important. The central bank must defend the peg by selling reserves. A speculative attack could quickly deplete reserves. The Greenspan-Guidotti rule is especially important: the central bank needs enough reserves to repay short-term debt to prevent a run. The import cover is less relevant for fixed exchange rate defense. The reserves/M2 ratio is also important because capital flight (residents converting local currency to foreign currency) can drain reserves.

This country's ratios (1.25, 25%) suggest it could defend the peg against a moderate attack, but a large attack could overwhelm it. The Asian financial crisis showed that fixed exchange rates are vulnerable when reserves are inadequate. Thailand had reserves of \$30 billion but short-term debt of \$50 billion (ratio 0.6), leading to collapse.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 1.25. b) 2.5 months. c) 0.25 (25%). d) Mixed: adequate on short-term debt, below on import cover. e) Fixed exchange rate increases vulnerability; reserves are adequate but not ample.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying reserve adequacy metrics to assess a country's vulnerability to a currency crisis and the impact of fixed exchange rates.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Bank Leverage and Capital Requirements

image.alt: Table: Bank A (assets $200B, equity $10B, leverage 20:1) vs. Bank B (assets $200B, equity $20B, leverage 10:1).
image.caption: Figure 14.5.10: Worked example: calculating leverage ratios and the impact of capital requirements on bank stability.

worked_examples.problem:
Two banks have the following balance sheets:

| Bank | Assets (\$B) | Equity (\$B) |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| A | 200 | 10 |
| B | 200 | 20 |

Basel III requires a minimum leverage ratio of 3% (equity/assets ≥ 3%).

a) Calculate the leverage ratio (equity/assets) for each bank.
b) Calculate the leverage multiplier (assets/equity) for each bank.
c) If assets fall by 10%, calculate the new equity for each bank (assuming no change in liabilities).
d) Which bank is more likely to fail?
e) How does the leverage ratio requirement (3%) affect bank behavior?

step_by_step.explanation:
Leverage ratio = Equity / Assets.

Bank A: 10 / 200 = 0.05 = 5%
Bank B: 20 / 200 = 0.10 = 10%

Both exceed the 3% Basel III requirement.

Leverage multiplier = Assets / Equity = 1 / Leverage ratio.

Bank A: 200 / 10 = 20 (20:1 leverage)
Bank B: 200 / 20 = 10 (10:1 leverage)

Bank A is twice as leveraged as Bank B.

Assets fall by 10%: $\Delta A = -10\% \times 200 = -\$20B$.
Liabilities unchanged (assuming no write-downs).

Bank A: New equity = 10 - 20 = -$10B (insolvent, negative equity)
Bank B: New equity = 20 - 20 = $0 (breakeven, technically insolvent)

Both banks would be insolvent after a 10% asset decline, but Bank A would be deeply insolvent (-$10B vs. $0).

step_by_step.step: Which bank is more likely to fail?
step_by_step.explanation:
Bank A (higher leverage, 20:1) is more likely to fail because a smaller decline in assets can wipe out its equity. A 5% decline in assets would wipe out Bank A's equity (5% × 200 = 10). Bank B could withstand a 10% decline (10% × 200 = 20).

This is why higher leverage increases risk. Investment banks before the GFC had leverage of 30:1; a 3.3% decline wiped out equity.

The Basel III leverage ratio requirement (3% minimum) forces banks to hold more capital. If banks were below 3%, they would need to raise equity or reduce assets. Higher capital makes banks more resilient to shocks. However, banks may respond by reducing lending (shrinking assets) to meet the requirement, which could reduce economic growth (the trade-off between stability and growth).

Post-crisis, banks increased capital ratios. The average leverage ratio for large US banks is now 6-7%, well above the 3% minimum. This has made the banking system more stable but may have reduced lending (contested).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) A: 5%, B: 10%. b) A: 20:1, B: 10:1. c) A: -$10B (insolvent), B: $0 (breakeven). d) Bank A (higher leverage). e) Higher capital requirements increase stability but may reduce lending.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying leverage ratio calculations to assess bank stability and the impact of capital requirements.

process_flow.process: Identify early warning indicators (credit growth, asset prices, leverage, current account deficit)
process_flow.description: Monitor indicators that predict financial crises: rapid credit growth, asset price bubbles, high leverage, large current account deficits, short-term foreign currency debt.
process_flow.mechanism: Credit-to-GDP gap (BIS); house price-to-income ratio; leverage ratios; current account deficit as % of GDP; reserves/short-term debt.

image.alt: Diagram: Early warning indicators (credit growth, asset prices, leverage, current account).
image.caption: Figure 14.5.11: Step 1 - Monitor early warning indicators that predict financial crises: rapid credit growth, asset price bubbles, high leverage, large current account deficits, and short-term foreign currency debt.

process_flow.process: Assess vulnerability to contagion (trade and financial linkages)
process_flow.description: Analyze a country's exposure to crisis countries through trade and financial linkages.
process_flow.mechanism: Trade share with crisis countries; bank exposure (BIS statistics); portfolio holdings.

image.alt: Diagram: Trade linkages, financial linkages, investor sentiment → contagion risk.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.12: Step 2 - Assess vulnerability to contagion by analyzing trade and financial linkages with potential crisis countries.

process_flow.process: Evaluate policy response options (lender of last resort, bailouts, fiscal stimulus, capital controls)
process_flow.description: Determine appropriate policy responses based on the type and severity of the crisis.
process_flow.mechanism: Lender of last resort for liquidity crises; bailouts for insolvent banks; fiscal stimulus for demand shortfalls; capital controls for capital flight.

image.alt: Diagram: Policy options (lender of last resort, bailouts, fiscal stimulus, capital controls).
image.caption: Figure 14.5.13: Step 3 - Evaluate policy response options based on the type and severity of the crisis.

process_flow.process: Implement crisis management (IMF programs, swap lines, guarantees)
process_flow.description: Coordinate with international institutions (IMF, central banks) to provide liquidity and restore confidence.
process_flow.mechanism: IMF loans with conditionality; central bank swap lines; deposit guarantees; bank recapitalization.

image.alt: Diagram: Crisis management (IMF programs, swap lines, guarantees, recapitalization).
image.caption: Figure 14.5.14: Step 4 - Implement crisis management through IMF programs, central bank swap lines, deposit guarantees, and bank recapitalization.

process_flow.process: Implement post-crisis reforms (Basel III, Dodd-Frank, macroprudential tools)
process_flow.description: Strengthen regulation and supervision to prevent future crises.
process_flow.mechanism: Higher capital requirements, liquidity coverage ratio, stress tests, countercyclical buffers, Volcker Rule.

image.alt: Diagram: Post-crisis reforms (Basel III, Dodd-Frank, macroprudential tools).
image.caption: Figure 14.5.15: Step 5 - Implement post-crisis reforms to strengthen regulation and prevent future crises.

image.alt: Master framework: Early warning indicators → vulnerability assessment → policy response → crisis management → post-crisis reforms.
image.caption: Figure 14.5.16: Complete framework for analyzing global financial crises.

summary: Global financial crises are severe disruptions in financial markets that spread across countries through contagion. Common causes include asset bubbles, excessive leverage, maturity mismatch, currency mismatch, capital flow volatility, and weak regulation. Contagion spreads through trade linkages, financial linkages, and investor panic (herd behavior). Major crises include the Asian financial crisis (1997-1998), the Global Financial Crisis (2007-2009), and the European debt crisis (2010-2015). The Asian crisis was triggered by capital flow reversal, fixed exchange rates, and foreign currency debt. The Global Financial Crisis originated in the US subprime mortgage market, spreading through shadow banking and derivatives; Lehman Brothers collapsed, triggering a global panic. The European debt crisis revealed flaws in the Eurozone architecture (no fiscal union). Policy responses include lender of last resort (central bank liquidity), bailouts (TARP), fiscal stimulus (ARRA), monetary easing (QE, zero rates), capital controls (Malaysia), and IMF programs (conditional loans). Post-crisis reforms include Basel III (higher capital requirements), Dodd-Frank (Volcker Rule, stress tests), and macroprudential regulation (countercyclical buffers). For Canada, the banking system remained stable during the GFC due to conservative regulation, but Canada experienced a recession due to trade linkages with the US. Understanding global financial crises is essential for analyzing the causes of economic downturns, evaluating crisis prevention and resolution policies, and designing international financial architecture.
